sentences.text: Oh, you did?
How do you make a list if you interview a person [INAUDIBLE]?
Well, as a small child, I remember only one of my grandfathers and his wife, his second wife.
He was selling flour.
And the type of a business it was, he didn't even have a store.
He just had few sacks of different flour at the entrance of an apartment building.
And people would pass by every day and buy a kilo or two kilos of flour.
We have to remember we lived at times were there was no ready-baked bread, so people had to bake their own breads all the time.
For some strange reason, I do remember fresh rolls, where everyone would buy every day, but not bread.
So that was the business.
That's how he made a living.
It was in Przemysl, P-R-Z-E-M-Y-S-L, in southern Poland, in Galicia, close to Carpathian Mountains.
It was a rather mid-size town.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the only child in the family.
I had a governess, who was with me all day long, from the time I got up until I went to sleep.
She watched me practice piano.
She took me to ballet lessons.
She took me skiing and skating, whatever there was else that I was doing.
Being an only child, I needed other children to play with.
And the governess would always follow me and stay with me while I was playing.
I was a rotten spoiled kid from a well-to-do family, the youngest in the large, large family, and everyone was doting over me.
My mother's name was Cyla, C-Y-L-A, Lewin.
Her maiden name was Liebschard, L-I-E-B-S-C-H-A-R-D.
My mother was a aggressive person, the go-getter, organizer in the business.
My father was very capable, but he rather had to have his rest every day.
Both of them worked beautifully together.
They have established a very successful manufacturing of the outerwear business.
Outerwear means coats, suits, jackets, and so on.
In my childhood, I remember living in a very small apartment with no running water, with very rough wooden floors in the kitchen, with a wooden-burning stove.
And we did have three housekeepers, one cook, and two to perform all the other duties.
We have to remember the water had to be brought in.
The wood had to be chopped.
Fire had to be started.
There was quite a bit of work.

The thing that I remember very, very specifically, I have to emphasize again and again the ingenuity of my mother, the upbringing for the future, because, when I was about eight, nine years old, I was taught everything there was to be done in a household, regardless of all the help.
Everybody got a day off, and I had to scrub the floor with my little hand.
I was crying, but that didn't matter.
The doors were locked, and I couldn't come out until it was finished.
I remember washing laundry on a scrub board and putting on top of the stove to boil the sheets with my little hands.
And then I had to take it out, rinse it, and hang it out outside to dry.
I was made to learn-- I was taught how to bake bread with yeast, all kinds of bakeries, how to make dough for the strudel with my hands; how to wash, clean, sew, darn.
All the socks had to be darned.
I even had to learn how to darn a damask tablecloth.
And if you realize what that is, it's a tablecloth that has shiny and dull designs on it.
I had to make a-- cover up a hole that looked exactly like part of the fabric.
And that was quite an art.
But at that time, every so-called lady of the house had to learn how to do it.
I was not allowed to ride a bicycle, because it isn't very nice.
It isn't proper for a lady to ride a bicycle.
I was supposed to write, play piano, learn literature, and things like that.
In my childhood, I was a very skinny child.

Vitamins were not available, so in order to get the iron into my system, a juice from vegetables, from beets and carrots and turnips, and various healthy things, was made, and I had to drink an eight-ounce glass every single day.
I also had to eat raw ground liver with onions with some seasoning in it.
It was terrible.
But in order to bribe me to eat it, I was getting $1 a glass or a $1 a little liver.
And that was a lot of money.
Well, it wasn't really $1.
It was zloty, which was Polish money.
After I saved all the money, I bought my own piano.
So that was how they accomplished getting some vitamins into my system.
The household was very, very well kept.
We had a routine every single day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was always catered to.
And I could do anything I pleased.
What makes it so interesting when you hear it later is when I'm suddenly left alone, I'm able to cope after being so spoiled.
And that's still a very big mystery to me.
It proves that a human being can adapt, and necessity is the mother of inventions.
You do help yourself.
My father's name was Eisig Lewin, E-I-S-I-G, Eisig.
L-E-W-I-N was his last name, and that was the family name.
My birth date is June the 8th, 1927.
Well, you have to realize I was a little girl going to public school during the day.
Afterwards, there were all the lessons and activities.
In the winter, I did an awful lot of ice skating.
And I was a very good ice skater, figure ice skater.
Then I would play with friends.
On the weekends, there were outings, some of them.
Now, our-- my parents were religious.
That meant that on Saturday I couldn't do very much.
I-- they went the services.
I was hanging around outside with other kids making all the noise.
But afterwards, we had to go home and eat the daily meal, the dinner, that was served in the middle of the day.
On Saturday, we weren't allowed to light the fire.
As a result, we ate what you called cholent, C-H-O-L-E-N-T.
What it means basically is any food that's available, it's put into baker's oven on Friday and cooks very, very slowly overnight.
And Saturday, after services, I would be-- they sent me to pick up the pot, the hot pot, and I brought it, and that's what we ate.

Into the cholent, usually luckily what was available at that time, there were a lot of potatoes, some kind of bones, a piece of meat, and beans and vegetables, which isn't much different than we have today, certain one-pot meals.
But potatoes were the-- was the staple of Poland, because we had a lot of it.
We ate sometimes two, three times a day potatoes in various shapes and forms.

In our city, there were two segments of Jewish community, the people that were very progressive and lived a modern life, and there were also certain people that were insisting on living the way people lived 1,000 years ago, the garb, the mode, the style, what they did all day long, just study and study the Bible.
There were some of those too.
Majority-- well, I really can't speak of figures right now, but we did have a substantial Jewish population in the city.
As far as I was concerned, I was a rebel.
I went to public school.
I spoke Polish.
I did not want to learn Yiddish or Hebrew.
First they hired a religious man for me.
And all I did, I pulled his beard.
And then they hired a young woman teacher.
And I was hiding under the table and running around in circles.
And I just didn't want any part of it.
I have no idea why.
I wish I would learn the language, because language is a language and you can use it.
I could use it nicely now.
But I haven't, and that was the fact.
Many of my relatives went to a day school.
And then they learned all kinds of histories and language and religious stories, I presume.
I don't know exactly what.
I didn't have any part of it.
So later now in my life, I am learning every day.
I am learning whatever I can learn about.
I was not aware at all of anti-Semitism.
Many, many of my friends were Catholic.
It's a Catholic country.
I was going to church.
I would go to the mass for Christmas, high mass at 12 o'clock.
And we were very, very friendly.
But I'm sorry to say, when the minute the war broke out, those friends didn't know me anymore.
I was like a pariah, like a cancer.
And they just abandoned me completely.
But I did have all the Catholic friends.
I do not think so.
No.
They were very busy in the business.
They did not belong to political organizations.
But there were quite a number.
There were all kinds of Zionist organizations, Palestine-oriented.
But my parents, as far as I can remember-- but you have to know, I was a child and they didn't want me to know.
I did not know about current events.
They almost protected me from that.
I didn't know what was going on in the world.
Would I have known, I probably would have acted differently, but I didn't know.
So--
Oh, yes, many.

Well, if we think about when one wanted to make a chicken soup, which was a typical meal, we had to catch a live chicken, take it to a schochet, who was a professional ritual killer, [LAUGHS] so to say.
And he killed the chicken very, very quickly.
Then we brought it home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took the feathers off, cut it, removed all the intestines, washed it, and then proceeded making it so-called 'kosher,' quote, unquote.
What that means is you put salt on six sides of the piece of meat, and you leave it there for one hour in order to draw out all the blood, and then rinse it off.
At that point, we were ready to make the chicken soup, and it was what we buy today ready in the market.
Today it's step one for us.
But in my childhood was a lot of work.
There was another typical thing when I was a child was a fish for Saturday.
We would buy a live fish and let it swim in the bathtub.
And I had a ball with that.
I observed the chicken-- chicken, excuse me.
Fish.
And on Friday, the fish was killed, had to be scaled, cleaned, and cooked.
And sometimes we just cooked steaks and sometimes we made what we call gefilte fish.
Gefilte fish means filled.
That's all it means.
We would cut out the pieces of meat from the steak of the fish, grind it like for a meatloaf, put some onions and bread crumbs and eggs into it, and fill the steaks back again, and poach it.
It was an awful lot of work, a lot of bones to cope with while eating.
But that was a quite a typical dish, because the fish that we used was very cheap and was caught in the local rivers.
It was carp, K-- C-A-R-P.
Today when we make gefilte fish, it's never from carp alone, because carp is very fat.
What we do, we use a little carp for the taste and add on white fish to it.
And we do make twice a year gefilte fish, but they're balls, not steaks.
We make them for holidays.
And the whole family here that's available now, whatever is left-- we call everybody a family that we know almost-- and they enjoy it.
All I remember from holidays, that my parents went to services.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to have a seat on the eastern wall was the most important thing.
Only the rich people could afford it.
The women were sitting upstairs and the men downstairs.
Women had very little-- they weren't recognized at all.
Women were not allowed to go to school.
Women weren't allowed to be educated.
Women weren't allowed to read the Bible.
Only the men could do that.
This was the man's world, man's privilege.
And-- afternoons in the holidays was the same like Shabbat, rest or study.
The men would go and study together.
They would gather in the room, four, five at a time, and the whole afternoon discuss various issues, whatever they decided to do.
Afterwards was a time to visit the family.
And we always visited the family Saturday and part of Sunday.
Now, we always had two days-- no, three days.
Excuse me.
I forgot.
Saturday only off.
On Sunday was visiting day, basically, and going places, picnics and so on.
Passover celebration really what it was was a whole month of work.
It was totally anti-women.

We had to take out all of the dishes, usually paint, clean the cupboards, get rid of all the bread and bread items made from bread, take out new dishes that were stored through the year, and eat only matzah.
The day before Passover around 9 o'clock in the morning was the last time we would eat bread.
And afterwards that all was allowed was matzo.
In the evening, we would have what we called a seder.
Seder is a meal where the story of the Jews from Egypt going through the desert is told.
Small children ask questions.
The families vary it at times.
But the best-- the seder that I remember the most is not in my childhood, and I have to tell you that.
When I arrived in the United States-- and now we are 1947, we going ahead quite a bit-- I had here two uncles.
One was very religious.
Here I arrived in big America all by myself, and he asked me to come for a seder.
Well, remember I don't know really how to read Hebrew.
I just know more or less the alphabet.
In the house he lived in were some very religious people.
Well, that service lasted about five hours, and I could not read.
All I did, I managed to read the last word on the page.
And I waited until they got to that word and turned the page so he's not ashamed in front of the strangers that his niece that came from Europe doesn't know how to read Hebrew.
And that was some experience for me.
Well, anyway, going back to the seders in my childhood, there was really nothing specific that I-- I can remember about that.
My parents had a very large family.
My mother comes from a family I think of seven, my father at least five.
Everyone was married and had children.
And all these people perished during the war.
When the war began, one relative, who was in college about 100 miles away in the city of Lwow, could not be located.
At that time, the Russians were killing everyone, flying very low, and killing everyone walking on the highways.
The whole family was positive that he was killed on one of those incidents.
He was the only one that survived the war.
The Russians took him to Russia.
He was in Siberia, and he returned.
Everybody else is not here today anymore.
That's the story of my family.
1939, Hitler decided to move east.
I presume everybody knows who Hitler was.
He was dictator in Germany-- that Germany is in the middle of Europe.
And he started occupying Poland.
Now, it took just few short days.
As a child, I was taught that Poland is the greatest and the best, most wonderful country in the world.
Later I found out, when Hitler invaded Poland, we had one airplane, which was taken apart for cleaning, and we didn't have any aviation whatsoever.
The Polish army on horses was fighting the German army.
Well, obviously, Poland was taken over in something-- was 10 days.
Germans came to about center of Poland.
Then suddenly the Russians started coming from the east.
At that point, my parents hired a wagon with horses, because that's all there was available.
And we decided to run to Romanian border.
When we were on the border already, we heard the Russians are coming.
Well, my parents made a very unwise decision.
And I hope nobody makes these kind of decisions again.
They decided to go back to save few more pillows.
The possessions were important to them.
Instead of running and getting out, because through Romania we could get out and come to United States at that time or to South America, they decided to go back, and that was the end of it.
They never came out alive.
So we came back.
Immediately the Russians [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started-- took away our business, naturally.
And in our apartment, the fellow whom we fed from a very poor family [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] decided that he's a Communist, and we're the capitalists.
So he came to our apartment, insisted that my mother brings the food to him while he's laying in bed.
He was-- had the satisfaction of making a mother-- a slave out of her.
And we fed him and his family for many, many years, but he felt he's s Communist.
Well, now, he's on top of the world, and he's going to be the big shot.
Well, it turned out that he did get a job.
He was a security man, and that's about it.
He had his disenchantment.
He didn't get any place.
He didn't become a big shot whatsoever.
But that was the propaganda that was existing, and that was it.
Slowly, the Germans started organizing the Jews.
It was so gradual that nobody could notice what was happening.
First, the Jews weren't allowed to work on civil jobs.
The professional people had to close their offices immediately.
There were only very few places where one could earn $1.
We were not allowed to walk on the street.
We had to walk in the gutter like slaves do.
Gradually, we had to bring all our possessions to the police department, like furs, jewelry, anything of any value.
That was announced on the radio.
I as a child, you have to realize, I had no television.
I was not allowed to go out at night and play with my friends.
But I did have a radio.
And I have to give up that radio.
When I gave that up, it's like half of my life would be gone, because can you imagine here today a child, a teenager, whatever, doesn't have any entertainment whatsoever?
It's like a prison.
Sidonia, I'm going to stop you there.
We're going to change the tape.
Gradually, at night they would be coming and taking people out, telling them they have to go to work, and people were disappearing.
It happened very, very slowly.
Nobody realized what was happening.
Nobody did.
You have to realize also that this part of the world was used to pogroms.
If anyone ever saw and there is a pogrom by the Russians of a small community, that's exactly the way it was.
And people would hide for a few days and then come out and it was-- it would blow over.
That means-- the reason I'm mentioning it, people were used to pogroms.
And they mentally thought that in 1939 was the same thing.
No, I do not.
No.
I never lived through a pogrom.
Most of the pogroms were in the east, in Ukraine and in Russia, and more towards east than I lived.
I lived in the center.
There weren't that many pogroms there.
But anyway, the mentality was such that it would blow over.
And that's why people did not take it very seriously.
They didn't stand up, didn't fight.
It was done gradually, slowly.
They said, well, we are not allowed to live in a certain section of the city.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We had to move to an apartment and share it with several more families in the ghetto.
Ghetto was an area in the city where they decided to put barbed wire around it so we couldn't move in and out.
Ghetto was a life of misery.
Very little food.
Dead corpses on the street every day, either from malnutrition or from disease.
Very few articles of-- of hygiene.
And the life was absolutely of idleness.
When that happens and people have nothing to do, people get in trouble-- and nothing to eat.
The food was-- a lot of the food was supplied by small children.
And that's very difficult for us to imagine.

What I mean by that is small children would smuggle underneath the fence of the ghetto, go outside, and swap for a piece of bread maybe a pair of shoes or whatever one possessed and bring it back for the family to eat.
The big holiday was when one was rich enough to have a funeral.
That meant that the wagon with the casket would go out of the ghetto and come back filled with food.
